Audio: All right, good. So good morning, everyone. Excited to cover a new topic. I guess I was just saying to the class here. I missed last week, which was simple. Hopefully you guys enjoyed The data processing the other set of tools on the data ecosystem. Normally I get to present those lectures but this time Rano was in Korea so I missed out on that. This week we're going to be doing logistic regression. Which is kind of like baby neural networks so it's really exciting. We're going to bring it a whole new set of models. There'll be a little bit of math maybe not as much as we had with regression. I will do some more code so it should be kind of fun mix of math and code today. So we're moving away from linear regression. I guess they should say we're moving away from regression. Because we're going to keep doing regression or say. We keep doing a form of linear modeling in a moment, but we're gonna we're gonna focus now on classification. And today we're going to introduce the kind of classification setting and we're going to derive the logistic regression model, which is the standard model used for classification. Video: 1: slido

text:
Audio: As I said, these are also the basic building blocks and neural networks. So, to first start talking about regression versus classification. So, up into this point, we've been building a... linear regression models doing linear regression and this picture I used in when I first taught this class actually in the machine learning landscape in the space of modeling problems are sort of let's say four main categories of modeling problems that people are counter so far we spent a lot of time on regression And you think this is a... I have labeled data so in all the regression work we've done so far we've had a why value. There's something we're trying to predict, so we had a label. We call these supervised learning problems. These are learning problems where I know what I'm trying to predict in my training data I have labels. And the label trying to predict is quantitative, uh, continuous. I'm trying to predict a real number. Like a salary. A temperature. Today, we're going to transition to predicting categorical data. making predictions about spam, not spam, and I'll elaborate it more on that in a moment. Throughout those next two weeks, two and a half weeks. We're going to transition from working with label data. two working with unlabeled data so it's not today but in fact not this week but next week we'll start looking at unlabeled data where i have for example Just some features, no labels, I understand is there some simpler way to represent these features? basketball = pd. read_csv("data/nba.csv")
first_team = basketball.groupby("GAME_ID") . first ( )
second_team = basketball.groupby("GAME_ID") . last ( )
games = first_team.merge(second_team, left_index = True, right_index = True, suffixes = ["", "_OPP"] )
games [ 'GOAL_DIFF' ] = games ["FG_PCT"] - games ["FG_PCT_OPP" ]
games ['WON' ] = (games ['WL' ] == "W") . astype(int)
games = games [ [ 'TEAM_NAME', 'TEAM_NAME_OPP', 'MATCHUP', 'WON', 'WL', 'GOAL_DIFF' ] ]
games

In this lecture, we will work with the basketball dataset, which contains information about basketball games played in the NBA. In the cell beld
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
cleaning to transform the data into a useful form, which we store as the DataFrame games .
Our goal in this portion of the lecture is to predict whether or not a team wins their game ( "WON" ) given their "GOAL_DIFF" . The variable "GOAL_DIFF"
represents the difference in successful field goal rates between the two teams competing in a game. A positive value for "GOAL_DIFF" means that a team made
more field goals than their opponent; a negative value means that the opponent made more field goals.

Our goal in this portion of the lecture is to predict whether or not a team wins their game ( "WON" ) given their "GOAL_DIFF" . The variable "GOAL_DIFF"
represents the difference in successful field goal rates between the two teams competing in a game. A positive value for "GOAL_DIFF" means that a team made
more field goals than their opponent; a negative value means that the opponent made more field goals.

In this lecture, we will work with the basketball dataset, which contains information about basketball games played in the NBA. In the cell beld
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
cleaning to transform the data into a useful form, which we store as the DataFrame games .
Our goal in this portion of the lecture is to predict whether or not a team wins their game ( "WON" ) given their "GOAL_DIFF" . The variable "GOAL_DIFF"
represents the difference in successful field goal rates between the two teams competing in a game. A positive value for "GOAL_DIFF" means that a team made
more field goals than their opponent; a negative value means that the opponent made more field goals.

first_team = basketball.groupby("GAME_ID") . first ()
second_team = basketball.groupby("GAME_ID") . last ()

In day 8 we did this weird thing which is actually pretty clever in retrospect. We said let's take our data and rather than fit a line because we don't yet know how to fit a line in this at this point in day 8. We said what we'll do instead is we'll break our data into these little bins like this. And in each of these bins, we'll compute the average of all this data, and then we'll give us some point. Yellows can see better. So there's some point here. Do that again here and you know, the bin here and so on. We'll connect all these points. We'll get some line that goes like that. what's what's the value within some interval. Right, we got this line here. based on our averages and that is actually pretty close to the regression line that we get if we saw off release resurrection. What if we do something like that here? Back in Data 8, you built up your understanding of linear regression by first considering the graph of averages. We construct a graph of averages by binning all x data
into bins of similar values, then computing the average value of y for each bin. This gives us a rough indication of the relationship between x and y.
Let's try this on our dataset.
[ ] :
# Break our GOAL_DIFF values into 20 bins
bins, cuts = pd. cut (games ["GOAL_DIFF"], 20, retbins=True)
# Bins contains the lower, upper values for the interval for that row
# Cuts contains the unique bin edges
I
# Lets look at a few of the bins
games.join(bins, rsuffix="_bins") . head ( )
We can visualize the bins:
[ ]: fig = px. scatter(games,
X="GOAL_DIFF", y="JitterWON", color="WL",
hover_data=['TEAM_NAME', 'TEAM_NAME_OPP' ] )
for cut in cuts:
fig. add_vline(x=cut, line_dash="dash", line_color="black")

For each bin we can compute the win rate within that bin. We do this by grouping the data according to which center of bin each row is assigned.
[ ]:
# Compute the bin center for every game (dot in the above plot)
games ['bin_center'] = bins. apply (lambda x: (x. left + x.right)/2) . astype( float)
# Now all the games that are in the same bin will have the same bin_center.
# We can group by bin center and compute the average of the label to
# get the win rate for each bin.
win_rates_by_bin = (
games [ ["bin_center", "WON" ] ]
. groupby ("bin_center")
. mean ( )
. rename ( columns={"WON": "Win Rate"} )
win_rates_by_bin
[ ]:
# Visualize the model
fig = px. scatter (games,
X="GOAL_DIFF", y="JitterWON", color="WL",
hover_data=['TEAM_NAME', 'TEAM_NAME_OPP' ] )
fig. add_trace(go. Scatter(x=win_rates_by_bin. index, y=win_rates_by_bin[ 'Win Rate'],
mode="markers+lines", name="Win Rate by Bin") )

The relationship between x ( "GOAL_DIFF" ) and y ( "WON" ) shows clear non-linearity. To apply the linear modeling techniques we've explored previously, we will
need to linearize the relationship. Recall our previous approach to transforming variables:
1. Apply transformations to linearize the relationship between our variables of interest.
2. Fit a linear regression model to the transformed data.
3. "Un-transform" our data to recover the underlying relationship between the original variables.
First, we need to decide what transformations we will perform. The S-shaped curve above doesn't resemble our bulge diagram, so we must apply a new
transformation to linearize the relationship.
To determine what this transformation should be, we make a few observations:
· All predictions on our curve are between 0 and 1.
To compute the average for each bin, we calculated:
# Y=1 in bin

= P(Y = 1|bin).
Together, these observations indicate that our graph of averages is actually modeling the probability of a data point having Y = 1! Rather than predicting any
continuous numeric output, we now want to predict the probability of a datapoint belonging to Class 1 (i.e ., the team winning the game).

The relationship between x ( "GOAL_DIFF" ) and y ( "WON" ) shows clear non-linearity. To apply the linear modeling techniques we've explored previously, we will
need to linearize the relationship. Recall our previous approach to transforming variables:
1. Apply transformations to linearize the relationship between our variables of interest.
2. Fit a linear regression model to the transformed data.
3. "Un-transform" our data to recover the underlying relationship between the original variables.
First, we need to decide what transformations we will perform. The S-shaped curve above doesn't resemble our bulge diagram, so we must apply a new
transformation to linearize the relationship.
To determine what this transformation should be, we make a few observations:

To compute the average for each bin, we calculated:
# Y=1 in bin

= P(Y = 1|bin).
Together, these observations indicate that our graph of averages is actually modeling the probability of a data point having Y = 1! Rather than predicting any
continuous numeric output, we now want to predict the probability of a datapoint belonging to Class 1 (i.e ., the team winning the game).

Cross-Entropy Loss
To select model parameters for our logistic regression model, we will need to choose a loss function. We might be inclined to use our familiar mean squared error. It
turns out this is a bad idea.
In the cell below, we artificially generate a "toy" dataset to play with as we explore the loss of a logistic regression model. We'll try to use the "x" feature to predict
the "y" target.

return 1/(1+np.e **- z)

return np.mean((toy_df ['y'] - p_hat) ** 2)

We've seen now that the fact that MSE is not convex for logistic regression leads to difficulty in optimizing .
Beyond this issue, the squared loss isn't well-suited for a probability task. Since p; is between 0 and 1, and y; is either 0 or 1, the squared loss for a single point
(yi - pi)2 is bounded between 0 and 1.
What this means in practice: Even if our prediction is terrible, the squared loss is never that large. Consider the "worst-case scenario" where the true class y; of
datapoint i is 0, and the model predicts a probability pi = 1 that this datapoint belongs to Class 1. Even though our model has made the worst possible prediction, the
squared loss is only (0 - 1)2 = 1!

Motivating Cross-Entropy Loss

We've seen now that the fact that MSE is not convex for logistic regression leads to difficulty in optimizing .
Beyond this issue, the squared loss isn't well-suited for a probability task. Since p; is between 0 and 1, and y; is either 0 or 1, the squared loss for a single point
(yi - pi)2 is bounded between 0 and 1.
What this means in practice: Even if our prediction is terrible, the squared loss is never that large. Consider the "worst-case scenario" where the true class y; of
datapoint i is 0, and the model predicts a probability pi = 1 that this datapoint belongs to Class 1. Even though our model has made the worst possible prediction, the
squared loss is only (0 - 1)2 = 1!

Motivating Cross-Entropy Loss
Let's look at a new loss, called the negative log loss, for when our true observation is 1. We define the loss on a single datapoint as l = - log p.

We've seen now that the fact that MSE is not convex for logistic regression leads to difficulty in optimizing .
Beyond this issue, the squared loss isn't well-suited for a probability task. Since p; is between 0 and 1, and y; is either 0 or 1, the squared loss for a single point
(yi - pi)2 is bounded between 0 and 1.
What this means in practice: Even if our prediction is terrible, the squared loss is never that large. Consider the "worst-case scenario" where the true class y; of
datapoint i is 0, and the model predicts a probability pi = 1 that this datapoint belongs to Class 1. Even though our model has made the worst possible prediction, the
squared loss is only (0 - 1)2 = 1!
[ ]:

-
p_hat_loss ["L2 Loss"] = (1 - p_hat_loss["p_hat"] ) ** 2
px. line(p_hat_loss, x="p_hat", y="L2 Loss", width=800,
title="Squared Loss for One Individual when y=1")
Motivating Cross-Entropy Loss
Let's look at a new loss, called the negative log loss, for when our true observation is 1. We define the loss on a single datapoint as l = - log p.

We've seen now that the fact that MSE is not convex for logistic regression leads to difficulty in optimizing .
Beyond this issue, the squared loss isn't well-suited for a probability task. Since p; is between 0 and 1, and y; is either 0 or 1, the squared loss for a single point
(yi - pi)2 is bounded between 0 and 1.
What this means in practice: Even if our prediction is terrible, the squared loss is never that large. Consider the "worst-case scenario" where the true class y; of
datapoint i is 0, and the model predicts a probability pi = 1 that this datapoint belongs to Class 1. Even though our model has made the worst possible prediction, the
squared loss is only (0 - 1)2 = 1!

We've seen now that the fact that MSE is not convex for logistic regression leads to difficulty in optimizing .
Beyond this issue, the squared loss isn't well-suited for a probability task. Since p; is between 0 and 1, and y; is either 0 or 1, the squared loss for a single point
(yi - pi)2 is bounded between 0 and 1.
What this means in practice: Even if our prediction is terrible, the squared loss is never that large. Consider the "worst-case scenario" where the true class y; of
datapoint i is 0, and the model predicts a probability pi = 1 that this datapoint belongs to Class 1. Even though our model has made the worst possible prediction, the
squared loss is only (0 - 1)2 = 1!

We can see that this penalizes wrong predictions far more than squared loss does.
How to read this plot: suppose the observation we're trying to predict is actually in Class 1. If our model gives an 80% chance of being in Class 1, the loss is relatively
small (around 0.25).
-
· If we predict only a 40% chance of being in Class 1, the loss is larger (around 1).
· If we predict only a 5% chance of being in Class 1, the loss is 3.
. And if we give a 0% chance of being in Class 1, the loss is infinite.

small (around 0.25).
-
· If we predict only a 40% chance of being in Class 1, the loss is larger (around 1).
· If we predict only a 5% chance of being in Class 1, the loss is 3.
. And if we give a 0% chance of being in Class 1, the loss is infinite.
What about when the true observation is 0? Consider the single-datapoint loss given by l = - log (1 - p).
